segments.title: Why modern warships keep growing
segments.summary: A live lecture explains modern warship growth as an unvirtuous circle of heavier weapons, sensors, wider missions, shrinking fleets, and concentrated capability.
segments.body: The discussion captures the stream's organizing argument rather than every later Q&A. Dr. Clarke compares a Flight III Arleigh Burke with a Fletcher-class destroyer, then identifies weapons, sensors, geography, threats, construction risk, and fleet shrinkage as drivers that push individual ships to carry more systems.

evidence.note: The opening comparison between Jack H. Lucas and a Fletcher-class destroyer identifies weapons, sensors, mission geography, threats, and construction risk as size drivers.
The unvirtuous-circle section links shrinking fleet numbers to ships carrying more sensors, weapons, systems, and crew, making each hull larger and more expensive.
The modern surface escort example explains how rail-gun power generation, missiles, hypersonics, and command-and-control systems drive a large hull.
